pursued [economic stabilization](/wiki/Economic_stability "Economic stability") and further political openness, while emphasizing its Western outlook and central European heritage. Today, with a growing regional profile, a participant in the [SFOR](/wiki/SFOR "SFOR") peacekeeping deployment in [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") and the [KFOR](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force") deployment in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and a charter [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") member, Slovenia plays a role on the world stage quite out of proportion to its small size.

first_paragraph: The **politics of Slovenia** takes place in a framework of a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), whereby the [Prime Minister of Slovenia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and of a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the [Government of Slovenia](/wiki/Government_of_Slovenia "Government of Slovenia"). [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Slovenia) "National Assembly (Slovenia)") and in minor part in the [National Council](/wiki/National_Council_(Slovenia) "National Council (Slovenia)"). The [judiciary of Slovenia](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Slovenia "Judiciary of Slovenia") is independent of the executive and the legislature. Slovenia is a Member State of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and is represented in the [Council of the EU](/wiki/Council_of_the_EU "Council of the EU") and through elections to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament").

The **politics of Slovenia** takes place in a framework of a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), whereby the [Prime Minister of Slovenia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and of a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the [Government of Slovenia](/wiki/Government_of_Slovenia "Government of Slovenia"). [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Slovenia) "National Assembly (Slovenia)") and in minor part in the [National Council](/wiki/National_Council_(Slovenia) "National Council (Slovenia)"). The [judiciary of Slovenia](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Slovenia "Judiciary of Slovenia") is independent of the executive and the legislature. Slovenia is a Member State of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and is represented in the [Council of the EU](/wiki/Council_of_the_EU "Council of the EU") and through elections to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament").

## Political developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Political developments")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Politics_of_Slovenia "Special:EditPage/Politics of Slovenia") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(January 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(November 2010)* |

As a young independent [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), **[Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia")** pursued [economic stabilization](/wiki/Economic_stability "Economic stability") and further political openness, while emphasizing its Western outlook and central European heritage. Today, with a growing regional profile, a participant in the [SFOR](/wiki/SFOR "SFOR") peacekeeping deployment in [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") and the [KFOR](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force") deployment in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and a charter [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") member, Slovenia plays a role on the world stage quite out of proportion to its small size.

From 1998 to 2000, Slovenia occupied a nonpermanent seat on the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council") and in that capacity distinguished itself with a constructive, creative, and consensus-oriented activism. Slovenia has been a Member State of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") since May 2004. It has also been a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") since May 1992, of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") since May 1993, and of all major international financial institutions (the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") Group, and the [European Bank for Reconstruction and Development](/wiki/European_Bank_for_Reconstruction_and_Development "European Bank for Reconstruction and Development")) as well as 40 other international organizations, among them the World Trade Organization, of which it is a founding member.

Since the breakup of the [former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), Slovenia has instituted a stable, multi-party, democratic political system, characterized by regular elections, a free press, and an excellent [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") record. However, Slovenia is the only former Communist state that has never carried out [lustration](/wiki/Lustration "Lustration").[[1]](#cite_note-1) By [Constitution of Slovenia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovenia "Constitution of Slovenia") the country is a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"). Within its government, power is shared between a directly elected president, a prime minister, and an incompletely bicameral legislature.[[2]](#cite_note-US2008-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The legislative body is composed of the 90-member National Assembly—which takes the lead on virtually all legislative issues—and the National Council, a largely advisory body composed of representatives from social, economic, professional, and local interests. The Constitutional Court has the highest power of review of legislation to ensure its consistency with Slovenia's constitution. Its nine judges are elected for 9-year terms.

In 1997, elections were held to elect both a president and representatives to Parliament's upper house, the National Council. [Milan Kučan](/wiki/Milan_Ku%C4%8Dan "Milan Kučan"), elected President of the Yugoslav Republic of Slovenia in 1990, led his country to independence in 1991. He was elected the first President of independent Slovenia in 1992 and again in November 1997 by a comfortable margin.

[Janez Drnovšek](/wiki/Janez_Drnov%C5%A1ek "Janez Drnovšek") of the center-left [Liberal Democratic Party of Slovenia](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_of_Slovenia "Liberal Democratic Party of Slovenia") (LDS) was reelected Prime Minister in the 15 October 2000 parliamentary elections. Drnovšek's coalition held an almost two-thirds majority in Parliament.

The government, most of the Slovenian polity, shares a common view of the desirability of a close association with the West, specifically of membership in both the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). For all the apparent bitterness that divides left and right wings, there are few fundamental philosophical differences between them in the area of public policy. Slovenian society is built on consensus, which has converged on a social-democrat model. Political differences tend to have their roots in the roles that groups and individuals played during the years of communist rule and the struggle for independence.

As the most prosperous republic of the former Yugoslavia, Slovenia emerged from its brief ten-day war of secession in 1991 as an independent nation for the first time in its history. Since that time, the country has made steady but cautious progress toward developing a market economy. Economic reforms introduced shortly after independence led to healthy economic growth. Despite the halting pace of reform and signs of slowing GDP growth today, Slovenians now enjoy the highest [per capita](/wiki/Per_capita "Per capita") income of all the transition economies of central Europe.

The Slovenians have pursued internal economic restructuring with caution. The first phase of [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization") (socially owned property under the SFRY system) is now complete, and sales of remaining large state holdings were completed in 2014. Trade has been diversified toward the West (trade with EU countries make up 66% of total trade in 2000) and the growing markets of central and eastern Europe. Manufacturing accounts for most employment, with machinery and other manufactured products comprising the major exports. Labor force surveys put unemployment at approximately 6.6% (Dec. 2000), with 106,153 registrations for unemployment assistance. [Inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") has remained below double-digit levels, 6.1% (1999) and 8.9% (2000). Gross domestic product grew by about 4.8% in 2000 and is expected to post a slightly lower rate of 4.5% in 2001, as export demand lags. The currency is stable, fully convertible, and backed by substantial reserves. The economy provides citizens with a good [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living").

Ten years after independence, Slovenia has made tremendous progress establishing democratic institutions, enshrining respect for human rights, establishing a [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") and adapting its military to Western norms and standards. Following this path it became a Member State of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2004. In contrast to its neighbors, civil tranquility and strong economic growth have marked this period. Upon achieving independence, Slovenia offered citizenship to all residents, regardless of ethnicity or origin, avoiding a sectarian trap that has caught out many central European countries. Slovenia willingly accepted refugees from the fighting in Bosnia and has since participated in international stabilization efforts in the region.

On the international front, Slovenia has advanced rapidly toward integration into the Euro-Atlantic community of nations. Slovenia has achieved two of its primary foreign policy goals: membership in the [EU](/wiki/EU "EU") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). Slovenia also participates in the [Southeast Europe Cooperation Initiative (SECI)](/wiki/Southeast_Europe_Cooperation_Initiative "Southeast Europe Cooperation Initiative").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/GovernmentPalace-Ljubljana.JPG/250px-GovernmentPalace-Ljubljana.JPG)](/wiki/File:GovernmentPalace-Ljubljana.JPG)

[Presidential Palace](/wiki/Presidential_Palace,_Ljubljana "Presidential Palace, Ljubljana") in Ljubljana.

Slovenia has been an active participant in Partnership for Peace (PfP) and has sought to demonstrate its preparedness to take on the responsibilities and burdens of membership in the Alliance. The [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") looks to Slovenia to play a productive role in continuing security efforts throughout the region. It has done much– contributing to the success of IFOR, SFOR, efforts in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and elsewhere– and has continued to expand actively its constructive regional engagement.

Slovenia is one of the focus countries for the United States' southeast European policy, aimed at reinforcing regional stability and integration. The Slovenian Government is well-positioned to be an influential role model for other southeast European governments at different stages of reform and integration. To these ends, the United States urges Slovenia to maintain momentum on internal economic, political, and legal reforms, while expanding their international cooperation as resources allow. Given Slovenia's membership of the [EU](/wiki/EU "EU"), many of these reforms are now legally binding through [European law](/wiki/European_law "European law"). NATO and EU efforts to assist Slovenia's military restructuring and modernization efforts are ongoing.

[Borut Pahor](/wiki/Borut_Pahor "Borut Pahor") has held the position of president since 2012. In November 2017, Slovenian President Borut Pahor was re-elected for a second term in close [election](/wiki/2017_Slovenian_presidential_election "2017 Slovenian presidential election").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Former prime minister Janez Jansa spent six months in prison in 2014 after being convicted on bribery charges related to a 2006 arms deal. Jansa had denied any wrongdoing.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In June 2018, the center-right Slovenia Democratic Party (SDS) of former prime minister Janez Jansa won in the [election](/wiki/2018_Slovenian_parliamentary_election "2018 Slovenian parliamentary election"). SDS secured 25 seats in the 90-seat parliament. A center-left party, The List of Marjan Sarec (LMS), was in second place with 13 seats.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Prime Minister Marjan Sarec resigned in January 2020. He led a center-left minority government since the 2018 elections.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In March 2020, Janez Janša became prime minister for third time in the new coalition government of SDS, the Modern Centre Party (SMC), New Slovenia (NSi) and Pensioners' Party (DeSUS). Jansa had previously been prime minister from 2004 to 2008 and from 2012 to 2013.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Janez Janša was known as a right-wing populist and an outspoken supporter of former US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Janša was also known as an ally of right-wing Prime Minister [Viktor Orban](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") of Hungary.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In April 2022, liberal opposition, The [Freedom Movement](/wiki/Freedom_Movement_(Slovenia) "Freedom Movement (Slovenia)"), won the parliamentary [election](/wiki/2022_Slovenian_parliamentary_election "2022 Slovenian parliamentary election"). The Freedom Movement won 34.5% of the vote, compared with 23.6% for Janša's [Slovenian Democratic party](/wiki/Slovenian_Democratic_Party "Slovenian Democratic Party").[[11]](#cite_note-11) On 25 May 2022, Slovenia's parliament voted to appoint the leader of Freedom Movement, [Robert Golob](/wiki/Robert_Golob "Robert Golob"), as the new [Prime Minister of Slovenia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia") to succeed Janez Janša.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Constitution")]

Main article: [Constitution of Slovenia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovenia "Constitution of Slovenia")

The [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") was adopted on 23 December 1991, effective 23 December 1991.

## Executive branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Executive branch")]

Main article: [Government of the Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Government_of_the_Republic_of_Slovenia "Government of the Republic of Slovenia")

Main office-holders

| Office | Name | Party | Since |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [President](/wiki/President_of_Slovenia "President of Slovenia") | [Nataša Pirc Musar](/wiki/Nata%C5%A1a_Pirc_Musar "Nataša Pirc Musar") | Independent | 23 December 2022 |
| [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia") | [Robert Golob](/wiki/Robert_Golob "Robert Golob") | [Freedom Movement](/wiki/Freedom_Movement_(Slovenia) "Freedom Movement (Slovenia)") | 25 May 2022 |

The president is elected by popular vote for a five-year term. Following National Assembly elections, the leader of the majority party or the leader of a majority coalition is usually nominated to become prime minister by the president and elected by the National Assembly. The Council of Ministers is nominated by the prime minister and elected by the National Assembly.

## Legislative branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legislative branch")]

The [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Slovenia "National Assembly of Slovenia") (*Državni zbor*) has 90 members, elected for a four-year term, 88 members elected by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") using D'Hondt formula and 2 members elected by ethnic minorities using the [Borda count](/wiki/Borda_count "Borda count").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/bd/Ljubljana_Parliament_Building.jpg/250px-Ljubljana_Parliament_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ljubljana_Parliament_Building.jpg)

National Assembly Building of Slovenia

The [President of the National Assembly of Slovenia](/wiki/President_of_the_National_Assembly_of_Slovenia "President of the National Assembly of Slovenia") is elected by the deputies and requires 46 votes to be elected. Currently, this position is held by [Urška Klakočar Zupančič](/wiki/Ur%C5%A1ka_Klako%C4%8Dar_Zupan%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Urška Klakočar Zupančič").

## Political parties and elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Political parties and elections")]

For other political parties, see [List of political parties in Slovenia](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Slovenia "List of political parties in Slovenia"). An overview on elections and election results is included in [Elections in Slovenia](/wiki/Elections_in_Slovenia "Elections in Slovenia").

## Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_Slovenia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main article: [Municipalities of Slovenia](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Slovenia "Municipalities of Slovenia")

Slovenia is divided into 212 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities "Municipalities"), of which 11 are urban municipalities with no greater degree of autonomy.
